title: A Critical Comment on 'Entropy Computing: A Paradigm for Optimization in Open Photonic Systems'
authors: Ali Hamed Moosavian, Bahram Abedi Ravan
abstract: In this article, we take a close look at Entropy Quantum Computing (EQC), a computational paradigm developed by Quantum Computing Inc. (QCi), which deviates from mainstream quantum computing by embracing rather than battling environmental noise and decoherence arXiv:2407.04512 . In their words this approach purports EQC as an open quantum system that turns "entropy into super-power fuels of its computing engine". We show that some of the claims in the main article can be made more rigorous, and yet these are still not good enough to beat state of the art classical algorithms on conventional classical computers. Note that these conclusions reflect the technology's current early stage of development and are not meant to discourage its pursuit. Continued rigorous exploration is necessary to fully assess the long-term viability and potential advantages of this distinct computational approach.
summary: This paper challenges the claim that 'Entropy Quantum Computing' offers a new computational advantage by showing that its performance on optimization tasks is matched or exceeded by standard classical metaheuristics. The authors argue that the Dirac-3 system acts as a physically motivated classical heuristic rather than a fundamentally new quantum paradigm.
main_problem: The paper critically evaluates the claims of computational advantage in 'Entropy Quantum Computing' (EQC) implemented via the Dirac-3 photonic system, specifically investigating if it outperforms state-of-the-art classical optimization algorithms.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the Dirac-3 system's performance on Max-Cut and non-convex optimization problems does not exceed modern classical metaheuristics like PSO, ECA, or Simulated Annealing, and that its observed successes can be explained by classical stochastic sampling.
method: The authors use numerical replication of the original benchmarks, implementation of classical metaheuristics in Julia, and theoretical analysis of the system's performance against known algorithmic bounds (e.g., Goemans-Williamson).
model_or_system: The system is the Dirac-3, an open photonic platform using an optical-feedback loop with an amplifier, mixer, and loss medium to simulate imaginary time evolution and sample from a Boltzmann distribution.
key_observables: Cut values (Max-k-Cut), success rates, frequency distributions of cut sizes, and effective inverse temperature (beta).
important_parameters: Low mean photon numbers (weak coherent states), effective temperature (beta), and the number of nodes (n) in random graphs.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the Unique Games Conjecture for establishing baselines and relies on the approximation that the system behaves as a Gibbs sampler.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the Dirac-3 schematic; Figure 2 illustrates the convergence of classical metaheuristics to a global minimum; Figure 5 shows the fit between the Boltzmann distribution model and experimental measurements.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a critique of the EQC paradigm, followed by a benchmarking of the Dirac-3 against modern classical solvers, a theoretical investigation of its performance on random graphs relative to approximation bounds, and a concluding assessment of its status as a heuristic rather than a new computing paradigm.
why_interesting: It provides a rigorous critique of a proposed new computing paradigm by applying tools from statistical mechanics and complexity theory to assess whether environmental noise can truly be leveraged for quantum advantage.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The schematics of Dirac-3. Image copied from [29], licensed under CC BY 4.0 (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/ ).
Figure 2: ECA and PSO Convergence on the non-convex polynomial g(x,y). The red line depicts the path of the optimum solution found in each iteration.

Figure 3: Comparison of calculating the Max-k-Cut problem on the Dirac-3 machine and on a conventional classical computer. The first row represents the graph we selected for this comparison; as we didn’t have access to the graph data in Fig. 4 of [29] we just generated a random graph with 30 nodes and 233 edges that has the MaxCut value of 146. The next two rows are a reconstruction of the Dirac-3 performance reported in Fig. 4 of [29] and do not represent the actual performance of their device on the graph in the first row. That is why instead of focusing on the cut value itself, the concentration of the distribution around the maximum cut values and the overall success probabilities...

Figure 4: The number of configurations that correspond to a certain cut value, averaged over 1000 random graphs with 30 nodes, 233 edges and Max-Cut value 146, Max-3-Cut value of at least 191 and Max-4-Cut value of at least 210. The error bars are one standard deviation.
